content.text: A plugin marketplace is a registry or curated catalog that makes Claude Code plugins discoverable, installable, and trustworthy at scale. The npm public registry serves as the primary distribution channel, community-maintained lists provide curation, and private registries let organisations control what plugins their developers can install — together they form the ecosystem around which Claude Code's extension community is built.
A plugin marketplace in the Claude Code ecosystem is any registry or catalog where authors publish plugins and consumers discover and install them. The term covers three distinct tiers:
The standard JavaScript package registry at
is the canonical distribution channel. Plugin authors publish there; Claude Code's
resolves package names against it by default.
GitHub repositories following the "awesome list" convention (e.g.
) aggregate notable plugins with descriptions, quality notes, and links. They act as a hand-maintained layer of social trust on top of the raw npm index.

Organisations hosting sensitive or proprietary plugins on npm-compatible servers (Verdaccio, JFrog Artifactory, GitHub Packages) control access, enforce package signing, and prevent developers from accidentally installing external plugins in regulated environments.

Unlike a browser extension store with a single centralized review gate, there is no Anthropic-operated approval queue for Claude Code plugins. Discovery and trust are distributed: npm provides the distribution infrastructure; community curation and source auditing provide the trust layer; organisation policy determines which registries developers may use.

Without a discovery layer, every developer who wants a deploy skill or a security hook must write it from scratch or copy it from a colleague's dotfiles. The quality bar varies, best practices are not shared, and the same bug gets fixed independently in a dozen copies. Marketplaces solve this by making reuse the path of least resistance: search, read a description, install, done.
For open-source library maintainers, a marketplace means contributors to a project can run

and immediately have the same AI-assisted review, test, and release workflows the core team uses — without reading a setup guide. That lowers the contribution barrier and standardises how Claude Code helps across the entire contributor community.

For enterprises, the private-registry tier of the marketplace ecosystem matters most. Locking developers to a curated internal registry means only vetted plugins can be installed, every installed package is audited, and the organisation retains control over what code runs inside Claude Code sessions — which matters when those sessions have access to production databases or source code.
Publishing to npm and installing from it both take a single command. Authors reach the whole Claude Code community without operating infrastructure.
npm's semver resolution means teams can pin to
and get non-breaking updates automatically, while a
major bump is opt-in. Rollback is
npm download counts, GitHub star counts on the source repo, and inclusion on an awesome list let consumers quickly gauge whether a plugin is actively maintained and broadly trusted.
Organisations that need to prevent developers from installing arbitrary third-party plugins can point Claude Code at an internal npm registry via
scoping. Only packages published to that registry are resolvable.

Because all plugins share the same npm distribution model, tooling built for the broader npm ecosystem (lock files, audit reports, dependency graphs, bill-of-materials generators) works unchanged on Claude Code plugins.
Anyone can publish a package with the

keyword to npm. There is no Anthropic-operated vetting gate, so malicious plugins can appear alongside legitimate ones. Download counts and community endorsement provide soft signals, not hard guarantees.
npm's search ranks by relevance to keywords and download count, not by correctness, security posture, or Claude Code compatibility. Finding the best plugin for a task requires combining
A community-curated list that was last updated 18 months ago may point to plugins that no longer work with the current Claude Code version, have been abandoned, or have unpatched security issues.

Hosting and maintaining an internal npm-compatible registry requires infrastructure, backup, auth integration, and operational process. For small teams this overhead may outweigh the security benefit of blocking external plugins.
To make a plugin discoverable on the public npm registry, the
must include the right keywords and enough metadata for consumers to evaluate it quickly:
directory is included in the published tarball — tests, development scripts, and CI configuration stay out of the installed package. The
field documents the minimum Claude Code version the plugin requires.
The primary search surface is npm's CLI and web interface. Searching for the
keyword returns all packages that declare it:

# Search by keyword
npm search claude-code plugin

# Search for a specific use case
npm search claude-code deploy
npm search claude-code security hook

# View a plugin's details before installing
npm view @my-org/claude-deploy-plugin
npm view @my-org/claude-deploy-plugin --json
command shows the description, latest version, keywords, and links to the repository — enough to decide whether to look further before running
to download and audit the actual contents.
Because plugins can register hooks that run arbitrary code on every tool call, auditing is non-negotiable. A safe pre-install checklist:

# 1. Download without installing
npm pack @my-org/claude-deploy-plugin
tar -tzf my-org-claude-deploy-plugin-1.2.0.tgz

# 2. Extract and read hook scripts
mkdir /tmp/plugin-audit
tar -xzf my-org-claude-deploy-plugin-1.2.0.tgz -C /tmp/plugin-audit
cat /tmp/plugin-audit/package/.claude/hooks/*.sh
cat /tmp/plugin-audit/package/.claude/settings.json

# 3. Check for known vulnerabilities in the package and its deps
npm audit --prefix /tmp/plugin-audit/package

# 4. Verify the publisher account
npm whoami --registry https://registry.npmjs.org
npm owner ls @my-org/claude-deploy-plugin
, or network calls in hook scripts, any reads of environment variables that look like secrets (
entries pointing to unrecognised npm packages. A hook that quietly calls an external endpoint is the principal attack vector for a malicious plugin.

Routing all Claude Code plugin installations through a private registry requires configuring npm's registry resolution. The cleanest approach uses npm scope mapping so the organisation's packages resolve to the internal registry while unscoped packages still resolve to the public npm:

internal packages can be installed as plugins, organisations configure the Claude Code policy to disallow external npm scopes. The specific mechanism depends on the Claude Code version and deployment model, but the outcome is that
fails with a policy error, while
resolves from the internal registry and succeeds.

The diagram below shows the three-tier marketplace ecosystem: the publish path from author to registry, the discovery and install path from registry to developer, and the quality signals a developer uses to evaluate a plugin before installing it.

attrs.alt: Plugin marketplace ecosystem: author publishes to npm or private registry, developer discovers via npm search or awesome lists, installs, and audits quality signals

content.text: When to Use Each Registry Tier
The plugin contains no proprietary logic, IP, or internal URLs.
The goal is to reach the broader Claude Code community — open-source project maintainers, developer tools teams, independent contributors.
The plugin is general-purpose enough that other organisations would benefit from it (deploy workflows, linting hooks, code review skills).
Plugins contain internal tooling, company-specific configuration, or references to internal infrastructure.
The organisation must comply with regulations requiring software supply chain control (SOC 2, ISO 27001, FedRAMP environments).
Developers work in air-gapped or restricted-egress environments where the public npm registry is unreachable.
How to Publish a Plugin to npm

# Verify what will be published
npm pack --dry-run

# The output should list only .claude/* files and package.json:
# .claude/commands/my-skill.md
# .claude/hooks/pre-tool.sh
# .claude/settings.json
# package.json
The README is the marketplace listing. npm's web interface renders it on the package page. A good plugin README includes:
A one-line description of what the plugin does and which problem it solves
A table of all skills the plugin provides, with their slash command name, description, and argument hint
A description of every hook the plugin registers, what event it fires on, and what it does (particularly for any hooks that block tool calls)
Any environment variables or external dependencies required (API keys, specific CLI tools that must be installed)
The Claude Code version range the plugin supports

# Login to npm (one time)
npm login

# Publish publicly (for scoped packages, --access public is required)
npm publish --access public

# Verify it appears in search (may take a few minutes to index)
npm search claude-code | grep my-org

# Test end-to-end from a clean environment
claude plugin install @my-org/claude-deploy-plugin
claude plugin list
Example 1 — Discovering and evaluating a plugin from npm

# Search for plugins related to database access
npm search claude-code database
# Returns: @db-tools/claude-db-plugin, claude-code-sqlite, ...

# Inspect the top result without installing
npm view @db-tools/claude-db-plugin
# Output:
# @db-tools/claude-db-plugin@2.1.0
# description: Skills for querying, inspecting, and migrating databases
# keywords: claude-code, plugin, database, sql
# maintainers: db-tools-team
# repository: https://github.com/db-tools/claude-db-plugin

# Download and audit before installing
npm pack @db-tools/claude-db-plugin --dry-run
mkdir /tmp/audit && cd /tmp/audit
npm pack @db-tools/claude-db-plugin
tar -xzf *.tgz

# Review hook scripts for network calls
grep -r 'curl\|wget\|fetch\|http' package/.claude/hooks/
# (No output = good; any hit requires manual review)

# If satisfied, install
claude plugin install @db-tools/claude-db-plugin
Example 2 — Setting up a private registry for an organisation

# 1. Start a Verdaccio private registry (Docker)
docker run -d --name verdaccio \
  -p 4873:4873 \
  -v /data/verdaccio:/verdaccio/storage \
  verdaccio/verdaccio

# 2. Create an npm user on the private registry
npm adduser --registry http://localhost:4873

# 3. Configure developers' .npmrc to route @acme scope to private registry
cat >> ~/.npmrc <<'EOF'
@acme:registry=http://verdaccio.acme.internal:4873
//verdaccio.acme.internal:4873/:_authToken=${ACME_NPM_TOKEN}
EOF

# 4. Publish an internal plugin to the private registry
npm publish --registry http://verdaccio.acme.internal:4873 --access public

# 5. Developers install from the private registry transparently
claude plugin install @acme/internal-tools
# npm resolves @acme/* from Verdaccio, everything else from public npm
A developer tools company with an active open-source community publishes several Claude Code plugins to npm under the
scope. They maintain three plugins: one for their CLI (adding skills for
), one for their hosted CI service (adding a hook that injects build metadata into every session context), and one for their SDK (adding
Each plugin repository uses GitHub Actions to publish to npm on every tagged release:

# .github/workflows/publish.yml
name: Publish to npm
on:
  push:
    tags: ['v*']
jobs:
  publish:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
        with:
          node-version: '20'
          registry-url: 'https://registry.npmjs.org'
      - name: Verify plugin structure
        run: |
          test -d .claude/commands || exit 1
          test -f .claude/settings.json || exit 1
      - name: Publish
        run: npm publish --access public
        env:
          NODE_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NPM_TOKEN }}
They include all three plugins in the installation instructions for their SDK getting-started guide:

# Install recommended Claude Code plugins for devtools-co SDK development
claude plugin install @devtools-co/claude-cli-plugin
claude plugin install @devtools-co/claude-ci-plugin
claude plugin install @devtools-co/claude-sdk-plugin

Within six months, the CLI plugin accumulates 1,200 weekly downloads — high enough to appear in community awesome lists and generate issue reports from external contributors. The company uses the GitHub issue tracker on each plugin repository as an async support channel, committing to respond to security reports within 48 hours and to publish a CVE advisory for any hook vulnerability.
causes npm to publish everything in the repository — including
files that look like credentials, test fixtures, CI configuration, and gigabytes of
if not gitignored correctly. Always specify
before publishing to verify the tarball contents.

It is tempting to install a plugin the same way you install an npm dev-dependency — run the command and move on. But a Claude Code plugin hook has more access than a dev-tool: it runs as your user, sees
JSON for every tool call (including file paths and shell commands), and can exit 2 to block any tool use. Always audit hook scripts before installation, regardless of the plugin's download count.
Not pinning versions in team onboarding

without a version pin install the latest version at the time each developer runs the script. If the plugin publishes a breaking change between two developers' onboarding dates, they end up with different skill behavior. Pin to an exact version (
) and update the pinned version intentionally.
Treating npm download counts as trust scores

High download count is a weak signal at best. npm counts include automated CI installs, package mirrors, and one-time curiosity installs. A plugin with 10,000 weekly downloads published six months ago with no subsequent activity and a single-author account is not more trustworthy than a well-maintained plugin with 200 downloads from a recognised organisation. Always examine the source, the repository activity, and who controls the npm account.
Automate publishing from CI, not from developer machines.
A GitHub Actions workflow triggered by a version tag ensures every published version corresponds to a tagged commit in the repository, making the provenance of each release auditable.

from a GitHub Actions environment links the published package to its source commit and workflow run, letting consumers verify the package was built from the expected code without trusting the publisher account alone.
Consumers upgrading a plugin need to know what changed. A
following Keep a Changelog format (Added / Changed / Deprecated / Removed / Fixed / Security) makes it easy to assess whether an upgrade affects skill behavior or hook logic.
Test against multiple Claude Code versions in CI.

The hook event schema and skill resolution logic can change between Claude Code releases. A CI matrix that installs the plugin on each Claude Code version in your declared support range catches regressions before they reach users.

to the plugin repository describing how to report vulnerabilities and what your response SLA is. Hook vulnerabilities (e.g., an exit-2 condition that can be bypassed by crafted tool input) require fast response since they affect every developer using the plugin.

Plugin marketplaces do not introduce runtime performance overhead for Claude Code sessions directly — plugins are installed once and their files are local. The main scalability concern is on the publisher side: a popular plugin with tens of thousands of users amplifies the blast radius of any bug. A hook that accidentally exits 2 for all
calls could simultaneously block thousands of developers from making file edits in Claude Code.

The practice of using exact version pins at the consumer side mitigates this: if version 1.4.1 has a broken hook, teams can pin to 1.4.0 immediately without waiting for a fix, while the publisher pushes 1.4.2. That isolation requires consumers to check for
regularly so critical security fixes are not indefinitely deferred on old pins.
Hosted private npm registry for GitHub orgs
Enterprise registry with scanning and SBOM

The npm supply chain has been targeted by malicious packages in other ecosystems, and Claude Code plugins represent a higher-value target than most npm packages because they run code in an AI assistant session with shell access. Key mitigations:
on any account that publishes Claude Code plugins. Account takeover is the most common path to injecting malicious code into an existing trusted package.
so each published version is cryptographically linked to its source commit and CI workflow. Consumers can verify the attestation before installing:

# Verify provenance before installing
npm view @my-org/claude-tools --json | jq '.dist.attestations'
# Should list a sigstore attestation linking to the GitHub Actions run
catches known CVEs in dependencies but not logic flaws in hook scripts. Manual re-audit is necessary when a plugin bumps a major version.

at the private registry level. Verdaccio and Artifactory support package-level allow/deny policies. Combined with developer machines configured to use only the internal registry, this prevents any unapproved plugin from being installed regardless of developer intent.
Think of the Claude Code plugin marketplace as the
npm ecosystem applied to AI assistant extensions

. All the same dynamics apply: popular packages emerge organically, community curation separates quality from noise, enterprises layer private registries for control, and supply chain security requires active attention. The only difference is the blast radius: a compromised npm CLI tool runs during build time; a compromised Claude Code plugin hook runs during every developer session, seeing tool inputs that include file paths, shell commands, and code content.
The right posture is: treat plugin installation the same way you would treat executing a shell script someone emailed you. Read it first. The install is one command; the review is worth five minutes.
The npm public registry is the primary distribution channel for Claude Code plugins; discovery uses the
There is no centralised review gate; trust comes from source auditing, provenance attestation, maintainer reputation, and community endorsement — not from download count alone.
Always audit hook scripts and MCP server entries before installing any plugin, regardless of its popularity — hooks execute arbitrary shell code in every matched tool call.

Organisations needing supply chain control should host plugins on a private npm-compatible registry (Verdaccio, GitHub Packages, Artifactory) and configure developer machines to resolve their plugin scope from that registry only.
Publish plugins from CI (not developer machines), enable npm 2FA and provenance attestation, and maintain a CHANGELOG and SECURITY.md for any plugin used by more than a handful of people.

Pin to exact plugin versions in team onboarding scripts and upgrade intentionally — semver ranges combined with a popular plugin's breaking change can silently alter hundreds of developers' sessions simultaneously.
before every publish to prevent accidentally shipping development files, environment examples, or test fixtures.
